subUnits.title: 12.1 Comprehensive Review: Economic Concepts and Applications
notes: Comprehensive Review of SSS1 Economics First Term:
• This week consolidates key concepts from Weeks 1-11
• Focus on understanding, application, and exam preparation
• Definition: Study of how societies allocate scarce resources to satisfy unlimited wants
- Wants: Unlimited desires for goods and services
- Scarcity: Limited resources relative to unlimited wants
- Choice: Selecting among alternatives due to scarcity
- Opportunity Cost: Value of next best alternative forgone
• Define economics and explain its scope
• Distinguish between wants, needs, and scarcity
• Calculate opportunity cost in simple scenarios
• Apply concepts to Nigerian context (e.g., fuel subsidy, education choices)
• Tables, graphs, charts for data presentation
• Frequency distribution: Organizing data into classes
- Mean: Sum of values ÷ number of values
- Median: Middle value when data is ordered
ASCII Diagram: Choosing the Right Measure
• Calculate mean, median, mode from given data
• Choose appropriate measure based on data characteristics
• Interpret economic data from tables and graphs
• Demand: Willingness and ability to buy at various prices
• Supply: Willingness and ability to sell at various prices
• Law of Demand: Price ↑ → Quantity demanded ↓ (ceteris paribus)
• Law of Supply: Price ↑ → Quantity supplied ↑ (ceteris paribus)
• Equilibrium: Where quantity demanded = quantity supplied
• At Pe, Qe: Market clears, no surplus or shortage
• Draw and interpret demand and supply curves
• Distinguish between movement along curve vs shift of curve
• Identify factors affecting demand and supply
• Analyze effects of price controls, taxes, subsidies
• Production: Creating goods/services to satisfy wants
• Factors of Production: Land, Labour, Capital, Entrepreneurship
• Division of Labour: Breaking production into specialized tasks
• Scale of Production: Small scale vs large scale
• Economies of Scale: Cost advantages from increased scale
• Identify factors of production and their rewards
• Explain advantages and disadvantages of division of labour
• Compare small and large scale production
• Analyze economies of scale in Nigerian context
1. What to produce? (Goods and services priorities)
2. How to produce? (Techniques and factor combinations)
3. For whom to produce? (Distribution of output)
- Mixed Economy: Combination of market and state
- Allocative: Producing what society values most
- Dynamic: Innovation and adaptation over time
• Explain how different economic systems answer the three basic questions
• Compare advantages and disadvantages of capitalism, socialism, mixed economy
• Evaluate strategies for improving resource use efficiency
• Fuel scarcity → choices about transport, cooking
• Education choices → opportunity cost of schooling vs work
• Rice market: Import policies affecting prices and availability
• Foreign exchange: Demand for dollars affecting naira value
• Agricultural production: Smallholder vs commercial farming
• Manufacturing: Challenges of power, infrastructure, skills
• Nigeria's mixed economy: Private sector dominance with state intervention
• Policy debates: Subsidies, regulation, public investment
• Focus on concepts and their applications
• Practice explaining ideas in your own words
• Relate concepts to real-life Nigerian examples
• Time yourself to build exam stamina
• Review explanations for questions you get wrong
• Practice drawing demand/supply curves, cost curves
• Calculate mean, median, mode, opportunity cost
• Label diagrams clearly with titles, axes, units
• For theory: Define → Explain → Example → Conclusion
• For calculations: Show formula → Substitute → Calculate → Interpret
• For diagrams: Draw → Label → Explain → Apply
• Read all questions first, plan your approach
• Allocate time based on marks (e.g., 1.5 min per mark)
• Mixing up movement along vs shift of curve
• Solution: Learn precise definitions and practice application
• Solution: Practice drawing and labeling standard diagrams
• Not addressing all parts of multi-part questions
• Failing to provide Nigerian examples where relevant
• Solution: Read questions carefully, plan answers, include examples
• Wrong formula, arithmetic mistakes, unit errors
• Solution: Show all working, double-check calculations, include units
• You have built a strong foundation in economic concepts
• Economics helps you understand and improve your world
• Your knowledge is valuable for personal decisions and national development
• Approach exams with confidence, preparation, and clarity
• SSS1 Economics First Term provides foundational concepts for understanding economic activity
• From scarcity and choice to production and resource allocation, these concepts are interlinked
• Nigerian context makes these concepts relevant and applicable
• Mastery of these concepts prepares you for advanced study and informed citizenship
• Economics is not just a subject—it's a way of thinking about the world
diagramDescriptions: Concept map linking all First Term topics: scarcity → choice → demand/supply → production → economic systems
Summary table: Key terms, definitions, and Nigerian examples for quick revision
Exam answer structure guide: theory, calculation, diagram question formats
Revision checklist infographic with tick boxes for self-assessment
Time management pie chart: allocating minutes per question type based on marks

quiz.q: The basic economic problem arises from:
quiz.explanation: Scarcity exists because human wants are unlimited while resources to satisfy them are limited, forcing choices.

quiz.q: Opportunity cost is best defined as:
opts: The monetary cost of a choice
The value of the next best alternative forgone
The total cost of all alternatives
The time spent making a decision
quiz.explanation: Opportunity cost measures what you give up when choosing one alternative over the next best option.

quiz.q: The law of demand states that, other things equal:
opts: Price and quantity demanded move together
Price and quantity demanded move in opposite directions
Price does not affect quantity demanded
quiz.explanation: The law of demand describes the inverse relationship: as price rises, quantity demanded falls, and vice versa.
Equilibrium is the price and quantity where quantity demanded equals quantity supplied, with no tendency for change.

quiz.q: A factor of production that earns interest as reward is:
quiz.explanation: Capital (man-made resources used in production) earns interest as its reward.

opts: Lower average costs as output increases
quiz.explanation: Economies of scale are cost advantages that firms gain as they increase production, lowering average costs.

quiz.q: In a mixed economy like Nigeria's, resource allocation is determined by:
opts: Combination of market mechanisms and government intervention
quiz.explanation: Nigeria's mixed economy uses both market signals and government policy to allocate resources and address market failures.

opts: Production occurs at the lowest possible cost
quiz.explanation: Productive efficiency means producing goods at minimum cost using the best available technology and practices.

quiz.q: A barrier to resource efficiency in Nigeria is:
opts: Poor power supply increasing production costs
quiz.explanation: Inadequate power supply forces businesses to use expensive generators, raising costs and reducing productive efficiency.

quiz.q: For exam success in Economics, you should:
opts: Understand concepts, practice applications, and relate to Nigerian context
quiz.explanation: Effective exam preparation combines conceptual understanding, practical application, and contextual relevance to Nigerian economy.
